summary: I grew up in Portland, Oregon, attending Elliot Elementary School until the third grade. Most Japanese Americans lived on the west side of the Willamette River, but my family was one of the few Japanese families in our neighborhood. My great uncle and aunt ran a small grocery store there, competing with Safeway. After the war, I stayed in Portland for a year while my dad trained in Los Angeles.
cues: west side of the Willamette River
great uncle and aunt's grocery store

summary: As a kid in Portland, I often went down to the Willamette River to throw rocks into it. Most Japanese Americans lived on the west side of the Willamette, but my family lived in a neighborhood with few Japanese people. The river was a familiar place during my childhood before the war.

My great uncle and aunt's grocery store in Portland competed with the Safeway just a block away. They managed to succeed by extending credit to mostly African American patrons and preparing fresh food like mustard greens and chicken, which Safeway did not do. Their small mom-and-pop store was a vital part of our community.

After the war, my dad went down to Los Angeles to get trained in machine work since he had no formal training with the different machines. I stayed in Portland for about a year during that time. Los Angeles was where my father improved his skills to support our family.

My grandparents were from Hawaii, but they all died before I was born. My parents left Hawaii in the early 1930s and moved to Portland. Hawaii was part of my family's earlier history before settling on the mainland.

My grandfather came from a small island in the Southeastern inland sea of Hiroshima Prefecture in the early 1900s. The farms there averaged about 1.9 acres, and after paying rent and taxes, farmers kept their profits. This farming knowledge was transferred when they came to the U.S., helping them succeed despite leasing poor farmland.

In California, Japanese farmers controlled 90% of asparagus and strawberries despite having farms under 125 acres. Their farming techniques allowed them to produce more profit per acre than other farmers. I remember getting strawberries from Watsonville and Salinas in August, showing the long growing season there.

Watsonville was one of the places where I remember getting strawberries in August, reflecting the long growing season in California. It was part of the agricultural region where Japanese farmers were very successful. Their farming methods made a big difference in the produce available there.

Salinas was another California location where I recall getting strawberries late in the season. This area was part of the extended growing season that Japanese farmers capitalized on. Their farms produced multiple harvests, contributing to their profitability.

I was seven years old when the Portland Assembly Center opened on May 3, 1942, and I was sent there on May 5th. It was located at the exposition stockyard, and we stayed until September 4th before being transported to Minidoka. The living conditions were cramped, with office-type cubes for rooms and little privacy.

After leaving Portland Assembly Center, we took a two-day train ride to the Minidoka War Relocation Center in south central Idaho. The climate was hot and sunny, very different from wet Portland. I remember exploring the desert, seeing rattlesnake skins, and the development of farms and livestock there. We lived in barracks with canvas cots and straw mattresses, and later my parents got seasonal leave to work at a farm labor camp nearby.

While I didn't mention much about Pearl Harbor directly, it was the event that triggered the evacuation and incarceration of Japanese Americans like my family. The attack on December 7, 1941, led to the government labeling us as potential threats and eventually sending us to assembly and relocation centers.

Before my parents were allowed to leave Minidoka for seasonal work, the FBI had to give them clearance. This clearance came through on December 24, 1942, allowing us to move to a farm labor camp near Twin Falls. The FBI's involvement was part of the security measures during the incarceration period.

At the farm labor camp near Twin Falls, Idaho, we lived alongside migratory workers who had come from the Dust Bowl days. These workers were mostly Mexican laborers contracted to return to Mexico after the harvest. Their presence showed the diverse labor forces in the agricultural areas during that time.

Mexican laborers worked on contract at the farm labor camp near Twin Falls, Idaho, where my family stayed after leaving Minidoka. They had 10% of their wages withheld to be sent to a bank in Mexico near their homes, ensuring their return after the harvest. This was part of the labor system during the war years.

I learned that Jamaican workers were also present as laborers in the farm labor camps, coming from England under the Lend-Lease program supporting Europe. They were part of the diverse group of transient workers alongside Mexican and Bahamian laborers. This showed the global reach of wartime labor programs.

Similar to Jamaicans, Bahamian workers came from England under the Lend-Lease program and worked in farm labor camps during the war. Their presence alongside other transient workers highlighted the international labor efforts supporting the war economy. We were called 'Evacuees,' while they were called 'transient whites.'

The Lend-Lease Act brought workers from places like Jamaica and the Bahamas, both English territories, to the United States to support agricultural labor during the war. These workers were part of the transient labor force in farm camps near Minidoka. Their presence was a direct result of this wartime legislation.

On April 7th, there was a significant meeting in Salt Lake City involving DeWitt, Eisenhower, and the ten Western governors. They discussed the distinction between internment and evacuation, clarifying the government's policies on Japanese Americans. This meeting was part of the broader wartime administration of the camps.

Milton Eisenhower was involved in overseeing the incarceration of Japanese Americans but struggled with the morality of locking up American citizens. He reportedly had trouble sleeping at night because of this and eventually turned over duties to Dillon Myer in June 1942. His concerns reflected the internal conflicts in the government during the war.

Dwight D. Eisenhower was part of the April 7th meeting in Salt Lake City with DeWitt and the Western governors, discussing policies related to Japanese American evacuation and internment. His involvement was at a high government level during the war. Though I did not have personal interaction with him, he was part of the decision-making process affecting my family.

Dillon S. Myer took over duties from Milton Eisenhower in June 1942 to manage the War Relocation Authority. He was responsible for overseeing the camps where my family was held, including Minidoka. His role was central to the administration of the incarceration program during the war.

Japanese Americans from Washington State were among the first to be evacuated to Minidoka. My family entered the Portland Assembly Center on May 5, 1942, and then were transported to Minidoka in Idaho. Washingtonians' early evacuation set the stage for the broader relocation program.

I attended Elliot Elementary School in Portland until the third grade. It was part of my early childhood before the war disrupted my life. I had friends there and did not experience discrimination at school despite being one of the few Japanese American children.

The Hirabayashi case challenged the curfew laws imposed on Japanese Americans during the war. I found government documents referencing this case and the special restrictions placed on Japanese Americans, unlike German or Italian Americans. It was part of the legal struggle against discriminatory policies we faced.

While volunteering at the museum, I researched the Minidoka Irrigator, the camp newspaper. It provided details like the Army's requirement to build fences and fire watchtowers at Minidoka in late 1942. The paper gave insight into daily life and security measures at the center during our incarceration.
